findings.title: Signed negative rows can increase without tripping the compare gate
findings.claim: Valid signed projection rows are mishandled by relative regression gating. Both implementations force delta_rel to 0.0 whenever before_pct and after_pct are non-positive, so a row increasing from -10% to -5% is marked stable despite a +5 percentage-point increase. This is new signed-compare evidence, not R2-07's scope-rendering issue.
findings.reproduction: Generated valid slice reports from v2 facts with total 100: before used values -10 for /neg/x.py and 110 for /pos/x.py; after used -5 and 105. Running `.venv/bin/clankerprof compare --threshold-abs 2 --threshold-rel 15` and the equivalent Rust command produced `{"name":"negative","before_pct":-10.0,"after_pct":-5.0,"delta_abs":5.0,"delta_rel":0.0,"status":"stable"}`, `"has_regression":false`, and exit 0 in both. The same output paradoxically lists the +5 frame under `top_regressions`. The zero is introduced at clankerprof/compare.py:117-123 and crates/clankerprof-core/src/compare.rs:81-87; gating then fails at lines 136 and 89-90 respectively.

findings.title: Duplicate top-level report rows remain an order-dependent compare bypass
findings.claim: Compare silently overwrites duplicate slice names using the last row. Reordering the same multiset of rows changes the regression result and can make the CI gate pass. This concerns top-level rows, not the duplicate function frames fixed by R1-03.
findings.reproduction: Used before `{"tool":"clankerprof_slices","summary":{"total_time_ns":100},"slices":[{"name":"hot","pct":10.0,"frames":[]},{"name":"hot","pct":10.0,"frames":[]}]}`. With after rows ordered `[hot:30, hot:10]`, both CLIs reported `has_regression:false`, `after_pct:10.0`, `status:"stable"`, exit 0. Reversing only those after rows to `[hot:10, hot:30]` made both report `has_regression:true`, `after_pct:30.0`, `status:"regression"`, exit 2. The last-wins assignments are clankerprof/compare.py:94-104 and crates/clankerprof-core/src/compare.rs:174-186.

findings.title: Finite attributable inputs can silently become null in Rust
findings.claim: A finite attributable metric can overflow only after scaling. Python then rejects the non-finite artifact, while Rust's `json!` silently converts the result to null and exits successfully, losing the metric. This is new derived-value evidence beyond I-02's input-type checks.
findings.reproduction: Ran scopes over two samples under the same `Scope` frame, values 110 and -10, categorized into separate Positive and Negative rollups. The YAML scope had `attributables: {huge: 1.7e308}`. This is a finite input and yields a valid 110% positive bucket, but scaling computes infinity. Python exited 2 with `{"error": "Out of range float values are not JSON compliant: inf", "ok": false}`. Rust exited 0 and emitted `"attributable_estimates":{"huge":null}` for the Positive bucket, category, and caller pair. Python computes the value at clankerprof/render.py:113-123 and rejects it at line 971; Rust inserts it through `json!` at crates/clankerprof-core/src/scopes.rs:576-589.

findings.title: Large valid integer totals break byte parity and by-slice selection
findings.claim: Python divides arbitrary-precision integers directly, while Rust first rounds each operand to f64. Valid large aggregates therefore produce different percentages and even different selected slices. Unlike R2-09, this is an arithmetic-value divergence rather than JSON float spelling.
findings.reproduction: Used two valid signed-int64 sample values, 4813636180488882346 in slice `a` and 3969268729998903787 in default slice `b`; total 8782904910487786133 is within the documented aggregate range. Python emitted `a.pct: 54.806880292428694`; Rust emitted `54.80688029242869`, so artifact bytes differed. Adding `--by-slice '54.806880292428694%'` made Python emit slice `a`, while Rust emitted `"slices":[]`; both exited 0. The differing computations are clankerprof/render.py:874/884 versus crates/clankerprof-core/src/slices.rs:293/381.

findings.title: Negative GC and uncollapsible pseudo-slices are silently discarded
findings.claim: Both implementations retain pseudo-output only when its aggregate is greater than zero, even though sample values are signed. Negative GC and uncollapsible costs consequently disappear from the slice artifact. This is a new slice pseudo-output surface, distinct from R2-07's scope rollups.
findings.reproduction: With one `(marking)` frame valued -10, both `clankerprof slices` commands exited 0 and emitted exactly `slices: []` plus summary `matching_time_ns:-10,total_time_ns:-10`, with no `gc` field. With one ordinary frame valued -10 and `--collapse 'path:/srv/**'`, both emitted the `(all)` row but no `uncollapsible` field. Python drops these at clankerprof/slices.py:435 and clankerprof/render.py:940; Rust does so at crates/clankerprof-core/src/slices.rs:271 and 350. The published slice contract requires both pseudo-outputs at docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:451-457.

findings.title: String-field validation remains divergent on three public configuration surfaces
findings.claim: The scalar-typing requirements fixed scope selectors but omit slice definitions, target configs, and runtime-rule match lists. Python coercion and Rust rejection/drop behavior change attribution across all three surfaces.
findings.reproduction: Three cases reproduced: (1) Slice YAML `paths: [123]` with frame `/srv/123/file.py` made Python attribute to `numeric` while Rust silently dropped the path and attributed to default `fallback`; both exited 0. (2) Target JSON `{"T":{"Numeric":123}}` made Python emit category `Numeric`, exit 0, while Rust exited 2 with `Target config pattern for Numeric must be a string.` (3) Runtime YAML `name_contains: [null]` on native leaf `NoneWork` made Python emit category `Hit` because null became `None`, while Rust emitted `Other` because it became `Null`; both exited 0. Relevant coercion/drop sites are clankerprof/cli.py:491-493 versus slices.rs:130-150, clankerprof/targets.py:45-47 versus targets.rs:94-100, and clankerprof/rules.py:135-141 versus rules.rs:195-208.

findings.title: Compare threshold flags accept different numeric grammars
findings.claim: The two public CLIs still disagree on finite threshold spellings because Python delegates to `float()` while clap uses Rust's f64 parser.
findings.reproduction: Against otherwise valid reports, `--threshold-abs '1_0'` and `--threshold-abs ' 2 '` both produced a normal comparison and exit 0 in Python. Rust exited 2 for each; for `1_0` it emitted `invalid value '1_0' for '--threshold-abs <THRESHOLD_ABS>': invalid float literal`. Definitions are clankerprof/cli.py:1498-1499 and crates/clankerprof-core/src/main.rs:261-266.

findings.title: The Rust guide still says scope parity is not claimed
findings.claim: The current Rust documentation contradicts both the shipped CLI and the parity matrix, violating the tracker requirement that public documentation describe the completed Rust surface.
findings.reproduction: `sed -n '63,66p;91,95p' docs/CLANKERPROF_RUST.md` prints `The Rust core should not claim that projection` and `Rust scope decomposition is not claimed`. In contrast, docs/CLANKERPROF_PARITY.md:88 says `Rust parity for scope decomposition | covered`, and `target/debug/clankerprof-rs scopes --help` exits 0 with `Run scope/cost-kind/rollup/owner decomposition over a profile`.

summary: Eight new reproducible findings remain: six major correctness/parity failures and two minor contract/documentation gaps. `./bin/dev check` passed, including its Rust lane. A separate `cargo test --workspace` attempt was blocked before execution by the enforced read-only sandbox (`target/debug/.cargo-build-lock: Operation not permitted`), so that was environmental rather than a repository failure. The tracker reports 71/71 done, but the cases above demonstrate uncovered requirement and test gaps; the worktree was not modified.
